summary: F-CS-11031512 - SSL Handshake Failures From One Region When Going Forward To Parent
executive_summary:
Issue: Meesho's ION config reported latency spikes and SSL timeout errors during March 8, 13:38 UTC which coincides with high traffic T20 WC event in India.

Mar 26
-------------------
Summary: Investigating each of the regions' pointed out by Akatech as problematic, identified different issues being the cause including Transient connectivity issue, Meesho's flit load overloading the machines. 
Provided recommendations on general config review which would help in managing the high flit load better.
Blocker: None
resolution_description: Provided recommendations on general config review
root_cause: Different issues being identifed to be the cause including Transient connectivity issue, Meesho's flit load overloading the machines.
creator: Ted Frost
mitigation_plan: Config recommendations are the best practices to be followed and it may help in improving in overall performance to an extent.
longterm_mitigation: Platform to detect spiky traffic and manage it effectively.

summary: [F-CS-11062418]-OOC mapping reported by Bell Canada
description:
Bell Canada reported their Canada user's traffic was getting mapped to AU and getting poor performance. 

Timeframe—March 5th 12:09 - 12:42p ET. In addition to this, the IAT for Bell Canada has mentioned the customer has observed similar problems in the past. The customer noticed OOC mapping for short periods of time in the past, which usually resolved itself fairly quickly, but repeated occurrences have resulted in an escalation from the customer side to look at possible options to prevent the sporadic OOC mapping occurrences.

Investigation for the March 5th instance: 
DNS IP: 161.216.137.1
MapID: e;[a.akamaiedge.net|https://a.akamaiedge.net/](152)
ESSL Slot: 215348 (Show all IP's) - Slot(s): 215348 - [www.bell.ca|https://www.bell.ca/]

 

Mapper results for 5th March show between Mar 5, 17:09:24 - 17:40:43 for around 30 minutes the requests were mapped to r50089 in AU. The score list also does not show any other regions and only r50089 that is in AU. 

[https://mapper.akamai.com/ip/#from%3D1772722560796%26to%3D1772733382796%26treeType%3DA%26mrule%3D152%26viperSlot%3D215348%26cidr%3D161.216.137.1%26resolver%3Dip%26useGMTTime%3Dtrue%26timeRange%3D%7B%22min%22%3A1772722560000%2C%22max%22%3A1772733382796%7D%26]

 

The PP assigned at that time was 67.71.247.210, which is in Canada. However, it has bad connectivity to various locations and shows losses

-[https://rome.akamai.com/cgi-bin/mview/mview.py?start_ts=3%2F4%2F26+15%3A52&stop_ts=3%2F6%2F26+18%3A52&pp=67.71.247.210&region=50896&loss_min=&loss_max=&rtt_min=&rtt_max=]

-[https://rome.akamai.com/cgi-bin/mview/mview.py?start_ts=3%2F4%2F26+15%3A52&stop_ts=3%2F6%2F26+18%3A52&pp=67.71.247.210&region=45836&loss_min=&loss_max=&rtt_min=&rtt_max=]

-[https://rome.akamai.com/cgi-bin/mview/mview.py?start_ts=3%2F4%2F26+15%3A52&stop_ts=3%2F6%2F26+18%3A52&pp=67.71.247.210&region=45836&loss_min=&loss_max=&rtt_min=&rtt_max=]

 

Another example from Feb 16 where 27504 from AU was assigned - 

[https://mapper.akamai.com/short/r4mxsUGiqB] 

 

Mapping for past few days looks good and consistent - [https://mapper.akamai.com/short/RVBFI3r1FO] 

 

As PP 67.71.247.210 showed consistent loss across multiple regions in the past, we are considering creating an NOCC ticket to mark this as bad. Please confirm if this is still needed, as the recent mapping results look good. 

We also need help answering this - why did the system pick a region in AU when the mapper could have picked any other major location in Canada or the US ? The customer understands the in-country mapping is not always guaranteed, but the concern here is why the traffic was mapped from Canada to Australia, which is geographically much farther away than any other closer possible OOC region candidates.
root_cause: Bad pingpoint had loss across regions impacting mapping.
creator: Sameer Gupta
mitigation_plan: NA
longterm_mitigation: Faster recovery from bad pinpgoints . Here took the CPD cycle (1day) duration to recover

summary: TESTING: Wrong byte range retry on purge sequence out of date error causing corrupted content
description:
The customer reported an MD5 mismatch for a recently uploaded file, which apparently happened before, too. The files are not in-place updated, and they're new uploads with new filenames. 
Example URL: [https://ispinext-prod.bmwgroup.com/BMW_ISPI_ISTA_SDP-RSU_4.58.84.istapackage.zip.001?stime=20260304224049&etime=20260403224049&outletid=4062610&encode=1a8f7805965aac8eaa9d6]

This time, we were able to reproduce the issue on 2 different machines and identify the hex bytes being different between the files that have different MD5sum values.
||Edge||MD5sum||Matches with Origin||
|89.27.247.13|df3c8041dbce5b452167bebe67e3d2bb|No|
|89.27.241.4|8cf90674d08ad025d1d72aa89452839d|No|
|23.52.66.134|a1568897324a4bbfac12b55f119dc64a|Yes|

The hex byte difference between all 3 tests starts at a specific point, which is the beginning of a new POC file.
{code:java}
[adidebul@lsg-gss5 /tmp]$ diff 3.list-incomplete 1.list | head -n 20
2991c2991
< #2991 bytes=6270484480-6270484499 0000000 d267 5766 db4b d1a8 b49b 046c 6004 586e
---
> #2991 bytes=6270484480-6270484499 0000000 4f8c 5969 446e 0a5f cea9 d328 bcd5 1efa <===


[adidebul@lsg-gss5 /tmp]$ diff 3.list-incomplete 2.list | head -n 20
2991c2991
< #2991 bytes=6270484480-6270484499 0000000 d267 5766 db4b d1a8 b49b 046c 6004 586e
---
> #2991 bytes=6270484480-6270484499 0000000 4f8c 5969 446e 0a5f cea9 d328 bcd5 1efa <===


[adidebul@lsg-gss5 /tmp]$ diff 1.list 2.list
9135c9135
< #9135 bytes=19155386368-19155386387 0000000 1b46 ce0e 9f9f dc07 58bb d3b0 6cf4 2cd2
---
> #9135 bytes=19155386368-19155386387 0000000 4f8c 5969 446e 0a5f cea9 d328 bcd5 1efa <===
{code}
We collected the logs and traced them to see what's different between these requests on that POC file request. 
POC request ending with byte range 6270484479 had a purge sequence error, but there was a retry. However, the next POC request (starting with 6270484480) had no retry on purge sequence error.
{code:java}
[seselvan@lsg-gss7 ~]$ zcat CP1558363-202603041600-23.33.25.197-814-1772738680.gz | grep "6270484479" | awk '$2=="f" && $1=="23.55.160.63"'
23.55.160.63 f 1772646970.104 2 3 0 4 0 1029 2048 10.55.160.67 89.27.241.4 GET /=/5084/1558363/1d/ispinext-prod.bmwgroup.com/BMW_ISPI_ISTA_SDP-RSU_4.58.84.istapackage.zip.001?stime=20260304170641&etime=20260403170641&outletid=406269&encode=10b4aeb1dd41bdfd3dc08 206 application/zip - TLSv1.3 ngPLktI 0 - - 8a87bed2 10.55.160.67 1445 1445 239 - 1c23cf66.6f40ca86 ERR_PURGE_SEQ_OUT_OF_DATE|httpReadReply - 116 174 - - - - - 0 2055 -;-;-;-;0.0;- 6268387328-6270484479 f7fb59b8a0c70.-1.n.g:m7fb59d039990.402765640.p.g:2eb8a890 - - 696 9664e880 - 0 0 - e9a8723a.198f1 %20Cg%20__slot157021____networkFF____levelL1____certCUS__ - mid=2006;mtse=g|0;sn=157021|;aid=995859;icp=bypass-cache;fastre=2;ulen=137:87;hnd=1;m_slv=TLSv1.3;pin=http|dod;ssl=3001301::::;aidv=42;netp=r;fp=270;rmap=mch-ff-lo.akasrg.akamai.com;mle=18855;vcd=3976 - - 0 - L1:L1::: - - ispinext-prod.bmwgroup.com
23.55.160.63 f 1772646970.111 0 0 0 28 27 2097152 2097785 172.235.167.92 89.27.241.4 GET /=/5084/1558363/1d/ispinext-prod.bmwgroup.com/BMW_ISPI_ISTA_SDP-RSU_4.58.84.istapackage.zip.001?stime=20260304170641&etime=20260403170641&outletid=406269&encode=10b4aeb1dd41bdfd3dc08 206 application/zip - TLSv1.3 noPtI 592 - - 8a870938 bmw-cos-prod.nl-ams-1.linodeobjects.com 896 896 0 - 1c23cf66.6f40ca86 - - 41760 83404 - - - - - - 0 -;-;-;-;0.0;- 6268387328-6270484479 f7fb59b8a0c70.269905.p.g:m7fb59d039990.402765640.p.g:2eb8a890 - - 125396 9664e880 - 0 0 8 e9a8723a.198f1 bmw-cos-prod.nl-ams-1.linodeobjects.com - mid=2006;mtse=g|0;sn=157021|;aid=995859;pin=http|dod;ssl=3001302::::;aidv=42;netp=r;fp=270;rmap=mch-ff-lo.akasrg.akamai.com;mle=15861;vcd=3976 - - 0 - L1:L1::: - - ispinext-prod.bmwgroup.com


[seselvan@lsg-gss7 ~]$ zcat CP1558363-202603041600-23.33.25.197-814-1772738680.gz | grep "6270484480" | awk '$2=="f" && $1=="23.55.160.63"'
23.55.160.63 f 1772646970.183 2 6 1 4 0 1035 2048 10.55.160.67 89.27.241.4 GET /=/5084/1558363/1d/ispinext-prod.bmwgroup.com/BMW_ISPI_ISTA_SDP-RSU_4.58.84.istapackage.zip.001?stime=20260304170641&etime=20260403170641&outletid=406269&encode=10b4aeb1dd41bdfd3dc08 206 application/zip - TLSv1.3 ngPLtI 0 - - 8a87bef0 10.55.160.67 1445 1445 239 - 1c23cff0.6f40ca86 ERR_PURGE_SEQ_OUT_OF_DATE|httpReadReply - 116 174 - - - - - 0 2055 -;-;-;-;0.0;- 6270484480-6272581631 f7fb580234bf0.-1.n.g:m7fb59d039990.402765640.p.g:2eb8a890 - - 696 9664e880 - 0 0 - e9a8723a.2ce8a %20Cg%20__slot157021____networkFF____levelL1____certCUS__ - mid=2006;mtse=g|0;sn=157021|;aid=995859;icp=update;fastre=2;ulen=137:87;hnd=1;m_slv=TLSv1.3;pin=http|dod;ssl=3001301::::;aidv=42;netp=r;fp=270;rmap=mch-ff-lo.akasrg.akamai.com;mle=18855;vcd=3976 - - 0 - L1:L1::: - - ispinext-prod.bmwgroup.com
{code}
Similarly, when comparing 1st and 2nd test at the bytes where they differ, we can see the same behavior where the edge didn't retry after it had a purge sequence error. 
Edge 89.27.247.13 went forward to cheu2f on a purge sequence error for POC file starting at 19155386368. 
However, edge 23.55.160.63 did not go forward on a purge sequence error at that same point.
{code:java}
[seselvan@lsg-gss7 ~]$ zcat CP1558363-202603041600-23.33.25.197-814-1772738680.gz | grep "19155386368" | awk '$1=="89.27.247.13" && $2=="f"' | sort -k 3
89.27.247.13 f 1772647625.245 9 17 3 32 0 1069 2048 23.55.161.67 89.246.242.3 GET /=/742/1558363/1d/ispinext-prod.bmwgroup.com/BMW_ISPI_ISTA_SDP-RSU_4.58.84.istapackage.zip.001?stime=20260304170540&etime=20260403170540&outletid=4062610&encode=14c908ea4aa00b293bf3a 206 application/zip - TLSv1.3 pa0tIM 0 - - 83a6fa5c mch-ff-lo.akasrg.akamai.com 1266 1266 239 - 479eb99e ERR_PURGE_SEQ_OUT_OF_DATE|httpReadReply - 116 174 - - - - - 0 2055 110029447;-;-;8613;0.0;-;0 19155386368-19157483519 f7f286fcdb2a0.-1.n.g:mc4605a6a0.470488518.p.g:12d78693 - - 696 2f462880 - 0 0 r e9a874c9.3be82 %20Cg%20__slot157021____networkFF____levelL1____certCUS__ - mid=2006;mtse=g|0;sn=157021|;aid=995859;fastre=2;ulen=138:88;hnd=1;m_slv=TLSv1.3;pin=http|dod;ssl=3001301::::;aidv=42;netp=r;qr=0|0|5000;fp=390;rmap=dscd;vcd=3976 - 5084.mch-ff-lo-sr2419.akasrg.akamai.com 0 - L1:L1::: - - ispinext-prod.bmwgroup.com
89.27.247.13 f 1772647625.297 9 0 0 49 54 2097152 2098128 2.16.10.167 89.246.242.3 GET /=/742/1558363/1d/ispinext-prod.bmwgroup.com/BMW_ISPI_ISTA_SDP-RSU_4.58.84.istapackage.zip.001?stime=20260304170540&etime=20260403170540&outletid=4062610&encode=14c908ea4aa00b293bf3a 206 application/zip - TLSv1.3 ptI 243 - - 83a5f3e3 a392.cheu2f.akamai.net 940 940 0 - 479eb99e - - 41760 83462 - - - - - - 0 - 19155386368-19157483519 f7f286fcd1580.-1.n.g:mc4605a6a0.470488518.p.g:12d78693 - - 125396 2f462880 - 0 0 r8 e9a874c9.3be82 %20Cg%20__slot157021____networkFF____levelL1____certCUS__ - mid=2006;mtse=g|0;sn=157021|;aid=995859;ulen=138:88;pin=http|dod;ssl=3001301::::;aidv=42;netp=r;qr=1|1|5000;fp=390;rmap=dscd;vcd=3976 - 392.cheu2f 0 - L1:L1::: - - ispinext-prod.bmwgroup.com


[seselvan@lsg-gss7 ~]$ zcat CP1558363-202603041600-23.33.25.197-814-1772738680.gz | grep "19155386368" | awk '$1=="23.55.160.63" && $2=="f"' | sort -k 3
23.55.160.63 f 1772670231.745 2 2 0 2 1 1033 2048 10.55.160.67 89.27.241.4 GET /=/5084/1558363/1d/ispinext-prod.bmwgroup.com/BMW_ISPI_ISTA_SDP-RSU_4.58.84.istapackage.zip.001?stime=20260304224049&etime=20260403224049&outletid=4062610&encode=1a8f7805965aac8eaa9d6 206 application/zip - TLSv1.3 ngPLtI 0 - - 8ad78fbe 10.55.160.67 1448 1448 239 - 1db86cbf.74a6041f ERR_PURGE_SEQ_OUT_OF_DATE|httpReadReply - 116 174 - - - - - 0 2055 -;-;-;-;0.0;- 19155386368-19157483519 f7fb5a3bbaa10.-1.n.g:m7fb59d039990.402765640.p.g:2eb8a890 - - 696 ba75b880 - 0 0 - e9a8cd17.b6089 %20Cg%20__slot157021____networkFF____levelL1____certCUS__ - mid=2006;mtse=g|0;sn=157021|;aid=995859;icp=update;fastre=2;ulen=138:88;hnd=1;m_slv=TLSv1.3;pin=http|dod;ssl=3001301::::;aidv=42;netp=r;fp=270;rmap=mch-ff-lo.akasrg.akamai.com;mle=18855;vcd=3976 - - 0 - L1:L1::: - - ispinext-prod.bmwgroup.com
{code}
Complete logs along with tests can be found in etherpad: [https://etherpad.akam.ai/p/F-CS-11033041]
 * Line 452: Spoofing tests reproducing the issue, and a direct test that gives the right MD5sum.
 * Line 774: Retrieving the hex bytes and doing a comparison between those files
 * Line 917: Comparing the 1st and 3rd tests to identify the difference around the POC request, where the hex bytes don't match
 * Line 993: Comparing the 1st and 2nd tests to identify the difference around the POC request

In both instances, the edge that didn't retry was 23.55.160.63; that edge did retry until the POC that matched, though. 
It's important to note that this is a recurring issue for them, and they usually purge to fix the issue. For this example, they've let the files be corrupt so we can further investigate. They're planning to purge tomorrow, so please let us know if any further info needs to be collected while the issue is still reproducible.

Ask: Can we further investigate why the parent didn't retry on the purge sequence error, but served a response to the edge?
executive_summary: The customer reported getting a download with a different MD5sum compared to the file at the origin. We were able to reproduce the issue with different MD5sums returned from different edges. The files had different hex bytes at different POC chunks. Logs showed no retry on a purge sequence error, but a response was served downstream, which likely corrupted the large file.
creator: Dennis John

summary: Tiktok slow handshakes in AS on 03/11/2026
description:
There was a Tiktok spike of SSL handshake failures and slow SSL handshakes on 03/11/2026 between 5:00 and 7:00 UTC in AS, but also in EU:

[link|https://grafana-jameswebb.akamai.com/d/b45e1eec-9ab8-4cef-927c-95fc1235d2cf/kpi-visualizer?orgId=1&var-kpi=ff_handshake_failure_c2g_rate_x1k_continent&var-kpi=ff_handshake_failure_c2g_rate_x1k_country&var-kpi=ff_handshake_failure_rate_c2g_hwtype_x1k&var-kpi=ff_handshake_failure_rate_c2g_x1k&var-kpi=sslhs_svchk_ff&var-kpi=sslhs_svchk_ff_continent&var-kpi=sslhs_svchk_ff_hwtype&var-kpi=ff_flit_overloaded_cpu_bound_hits_cont_pctx1k_new&var-key_regex=.&from=1773107222000&to=1773315870000&viewPanel=8]

resolution_description: SI-00023558 - The issue was observed between 5:15 and 7:11 UTC ONLY. This issue has impacted multiple customers who would have observed latency and DoS as 2.4% of ESSL and 1.7% of FF traffic was served from overloaded machines. The impact window is from 5:15am UTC to 7:11am UTC.
root_cause: RA output check failed - SI-00023558.
creator: Vlad Slavici
mitigation_plan: Updates on SI-00023558.
longterm_mitigation: TBD as per SI-00023558.

summary: Daily peak flit spikes in r53419 causing increased latency for Amazon
description:
Customer reported that they noticed increased latency in the IAD region daily around 1 UTC. We reviewed the logs, and we noticed intermittent spikes in the number of requests taking more than 5 seconds. 

Checking the edge region/ IPs showing high response time, we noticed r53419 standing out with a high number of requests. We also see multiple machines with high latency for r53419.
{code:java}
[fsalazar@lsg-gss13 ~/F-CS-11072915]$ zcat CP1642358-202603110030-23.33.25.197-356-1773696623.gz | awk '2=="r" && !($16~/[fGKPX]/ || $53~/m/) && !($11~/^(10\.|127\.0\.0\.1)/) && $5+$6+$7>4000 {print $1}; $2=="S" && !($18~/[fGKPX]/ ) && !($11~/^(10\.|127\.0\.0\.1)/) && $5+$6+$7>4000 {print $1}' | xargs region | awk '{print $8, $9, $10}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
   1478 region 53419 (FABRIC-IAD51j)
     69 region 49579 (FABRIC-MIA22s)
     29 region 49926 (FABRIC-MIA32b)
     17 region 49689 (SDN-YTO1c)
     16 region 51472 (FABRIC-ATL4c)
     14 region 51174 (FABRIC-DFW14g)
     13 region 9987 (UNSACV-MTY2)
     13 region 50535 (FABRIC-GRU12h)
     11 region 44133 (SDN-QROf)
     10 region 49257 (FABRIC-EWR43j)
     10 region 41964 (IZZI-MTY)

[seselvan@lsg-gss13 /home/fsalazar/F-CS-11072915]$ zcat CP1642358-202603110030-23.33.25.197-356-1773696623.gz | awk '2=="r" && !($16~/[fGKPX]/ || $53~/m/) && !($11~/^(10\.|127\.0\.0\.1)/) && $5+$6+$7>4000 {print $1}; $2=="S" && !($18~/[fGKPX]/ ) && !($11~/^(10\.|127\.0\.0\.1)/) && $5+$6+$7>4000 {print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head
     46 2.17.114.13
     44 23.7.250.203
     42 2.17.114.30
     40 2.17.114.29
     39 23.7.250.145
     34 2.17.114.201
     32 23.7.250.222
     31 2.17.114.5
     29 2.17.114.208
     28 2.17.114.28
{code}

We reviewed a couple of log lines (segment and manifest requests), and we see a high request end time, even going forward to peer. We also saw zero-size object errors from different peers.
Sample logs can be found in etherpad: https://etherpad.akam.ai/p/F-CS-11072915_SPOE

[Loadgraph shows daily flit increases|https://lg.netarch.akamai.com/cgi-bin/loadgraphs?period=1w&region=53419&network=A&maprule=&display=total&ymax=&utcoffset=-6&diskcpu=on] with some self-suspensions from 02/27 when this region started getting heavy traffic. Looking back, it looks like most of the machines were brought online on 02/13 for this large region, based on [Netarch|https://netarch.akamai.com/region/FABRIC-IAD51j]. 

[Mapper historical grapher|https://map.akamai.com/cgi-bin/FreeFlow/Analysis/HDB_Server.pl?startmonth=3&startday=10&startyear=2026&starthour=22&startmin=0&endmonth=3&endday=11&endyear=2026&endhour=4&endmin=0&param1=53419&dbname=production&param2=&param3=&param4=&display=graph&normalize=none&ptdisp=normal&yaxis=y-axis&size=large&scope=all&query_name=REGION_BY_MAPRULE_FLYTELOAD&customSql=] shows high flit from MR 122 (Hulu edge map) followed by MR 19 (ByteDance US-only video edge map). This can be seen yesterday during peak hours as well. 

We pulled ghost and cache.log for one of the machines, and we can see CPU, flit and AVG manager cycle having spikes intermittently for this region, aligning with intermittent latency spikes. https://deadpool.akamai.com/edgelogminer/modern-view?task_id=fd701275-7db1-46fd-87f5-f6bbba1ca8ee

Reviewing top CPcodes, we see Hulu cpcode 1628770 (part of Disney account) having big spikes compared to other CPcodes during that time. 
 !image-2026-03-17-18-09-45-754.png|width=100%! 

{code:java}
[seselvan@lsg-gss6 ~]$ cpcode 1628770
cpcode      : 1628770
cpname      : hulu.vod
contentcode : 1628770
contentname : hulu.vod
clientname  : W-O8K658
masteracg   : W-O8K658
accountname : Disney Streaming Services
tier        : A+
{code}

Looking at FOM, we see this region has 0.8, so should we reduce FOM to mitigate these daily peaks of flit overload, causing latency for Amazon?

{code:java}
sg-gss13 ~/F-CS-11072915]$ date;agg map-dev "select region, flit_overflow_mark from m2RegionOverflowMarks where region IN (53419)"
Tue Mar 17 17:40:47 UTC 2026
region flit_overflow_mark
------ ------------------
53419 0.8
{code}

Also, are there any actions that can be taken to prevent similar issues in the future? Let us know if you need any further info/ analysis.
executive_summary: Customer reported that they noticed increased latency in the IAD region daily around 1 UTC. We reviewed the logs, and we noticed intermittent spikes in slow requests mainly from r53419. We see CPU/ flit and AVG manager cycle having intermittent spikes during that time. Should we reduce FOM to mitigate the issue? Are there any actions that can be taken to prevent similar issues in the future?
resolution_description:
Kernel team to roll out a kernel tweak and for a switch firmware fix which should address the root cause.
The ETA for the kernel rollout, before 03/27.
root_cause: r53419 ahve the backend less problem which leads to ghostmon suspending ghosts which don’t need to be suspended.
creator: Senthuran Selvanayagam
mitigation_plan:
Kernel team to roll out a kernel tweak and a switch firmware fix.
The ETA for the kernel rollout, before 03/27
longterm_mitigation: NA

summary: {F-CS-11072302} - Edge retrying SR alternate paths on a 504 response from Origin
description:
* Customer: Ryt bank Malaysia - F-AC-5056189
 * Property: mobileapp-prod

Customer has performance SR + OIPACL but no failover/failaction behaviors.

When getting a 504 from Origin, we see SR flag the parent as dead(F - if the request failed Sureroute parent type ) and then retrying alternate paths & RA fallback.

{noformat}
My main concern here is, should we even be retrying alternate paths for an explicit 504 from Origin?. We connected and got a response, so there were no network/path issues and yet SR is retrying. That can't be intended behavior

Can you confirm that this is unintended behavior?. What would be the best way to stop the alternate path retries  for an Origin 5xx in this scenario?.

I suspect this might've been introduced due to the fix on DPEREQ-355  and [corresponding rollout |https://collaborate.akamai.com/confluence/spaces/DynamicMedia/pages/1257888826/Rollout+Plan+Introduce+HTTP+Status+Codes+to+Origin+IP+ACL+Acceptance+Criteria]

Also I know it's a bit of a gray area. Let me know if I should open a SRMM ticket for the SR folks instead. Thanks!.

===

Another sample here: [https://etherpad.akam.ai/p/ER-263671]
Full cachelog for all machines reqID 3889dbef   : LSG 14 - /home/jpadilla/ER-263671/
executive_summary: So the retries on 504 are as expected due to the change deployed as a part of DPEREQ-355 and the ghost does not have a way to selectively apply fallback using acceptance-criteria based on the source of the 5xx status code. We never expect a Ghost to be entirely offline, if we receive a 504 from upstream (whether origin or parent) and the 504 is configured in metadata to mean that parent route is dead, then that is how it goes further.
root_cause: The retries on 504 are as expected due to the change deployed as a part of DPEREQ-355 and the ghost does not have a way to selectively apply fallback using acceptance-criteria based on the source of the 5xx status code.
creator: Javier Padilla
mitigation_plan: Nothing from ERE side
longterm_mitigation: Nothing from ERE side

summary: Zomato impacted due to hotstar API traffic spikes and preventive actions
description:
Customers experienced  high rate of SSL handshake failures (ERR_FWD_SSL_HANDSHAKE | ERR_CONN_STRICT_CERT) for the hostnames:
 * api.zomato.com
 * api.runnr.in

Time window:
 * Start: 2026-03-08 14:49:42 GMT
 * End: 2026-03-08 14:50:02 GMT

Duration: ~20 seconds of total service disruption

Impact was observed due to midgress region r35481, due to spike in traffic from _bifrost-api.hotstar.com_ during the IND vs NZ final match.

Loadgraph : [https://www.nocc.akamai.com/miniurl/?id=298d7]
{code:java}
zcat CP1570382-202603081355-23.33.25.197-917-1772987481.gz |awk '$2=="f" && $19!~/o/ && $30~"ERR_FWD_SSL_HANDSHAKE" {print $11}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head

  15500 23.214.85.131

ghost_grep --log-file=ghostmon.log --use-normandy --range=03/08/2026/14:45-03/08/2026/14:50 --by-region "//|  " 35481 | gzip -c > _35481_03.08.2026.14.45-03.08.2026.14.50.log.gz
[snes@lsg-gss7 ~]$ zcat _35481_03.08.2026.14.45-03.08.2026.14.50.log.gz | grep 'suspendlevel' | awk '{print $10,$11}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head

The child edge maprule contributing to load on region is 5697 ( a;ch7.akamai.net ) [check here|https://mapper.akamai.com/dnsp/serial/#from%3D1772975400000%26to%3D1772987760000%26dataFrom%3D1772987760000%26useGMTTime%3Dtrue%26timeRange%3D%7B%22min%22%3A1772975400000%2C%22max%22%3A1772987760000%7D%26regionId%3D35481%26service%3DW%26serial%3D2047%26]

Top hostnames were hotstar domains.
{code:java}
cat 14_49_log |  awk '$1=="f" {print $62} '|time_convert | sort | uniq -c |sort -rn    82050 bifrost-api.hotstar.com

  80 live12p.akt.hotstar-cdn.net

  57 z5ak-cmaflive.zee5.com

cat 14_50.log | awk '$1=="f" {print $62} '|time_convert | sort | uniq -c |sort -rn
  78334 bifrost-api.hotstar.com
   9736 v16m.tiktokcdn.com
   7142 api.zomato.com

Although auto-suspension mechanisms triggered and redirected traffic to other clusters. There was a transition delay (~20–30 seconds) for traffic rerouting (“ghost redirect”) .

 

Etherpad :  [https://etherpad.akam.ai/p/F-CS-11044022]

Analysis : [https://docs.google.com/document/d/1WyU-Gw1wuUrKOPbT6McN2FSMx6oxarDVB8att12DMQ8/edit?tab=t.0]

 

Ask ERE,

1) The impacted region (r35481) was not included in pre-configured FOM (Flit Overflow Mark) reductions, unlike other high-traffic regions.

region  flit_overflow_mark

------  ------------------

 35481                 0.8


2) Any mitigations that can help to reduce future impact due to upcoming IPL events.
resolution_description: Config reviewed and provided recommendations to help improving the performance in general.
root_cause: High flit load on the region caused the latency spikes but with the logs available, not sure of the cpcode/config with spiky flit load.
creator: Archanna Sivakumar
mitigation_plan: Config reviewed and provided recommendations to help improving the performance in general.
longterm_mitigation: Platform to detect spiky traffic and manage it effectively.

summary: High RET on EW Subrequest http call
description:
Hello Folks
Affected hostname : webmail.cma-cgm.com
 
Context: Customer is using third party service called DataDome (similar to BMP). The request flow looks like as follows: (Attaching snapshot as well)
1) Client makes a request to webmail.cma-cgm.com
2) Request reached Edge and according Edge worker configuration another request is being made for /validate-request path (3rd party BOTMAN solution)
3) This /validate-request path reaches datadome (origin), it validates and serves the response (deny or allow request)
4) Based on this the original request is either allowed go to origin or send response back to client.
 
Concern:
The customer has started observing elevated latency for approximately 1% of the requests reaching their DataDome service.
 
The IAT team disabled SiteShield to remove the additional hop that could have been contributing to latency. Following this change, response times improved for the 50th, 75th, and 95th percentiles. However, the 99th percentile—representing roughly 1% of requests—continues to show elevated latency.
 
Metric/Benchmark of concern: Requests that are taking more than 300 ms. This creates a security hole for the customer - the 300 ms is a limit set at the EW code itself, if the request coming from a client takes more than 300ms to get a response from DD , their EW code considers it as a fail open.


Below is one sample request that takes too long with :
 
```
Request end time : 66656
 EdgeWorker Billing Information (ewb) //89800/-/0/100/1024
      Event Handler Execution 89800/-/0/100/1024
         EdgeWorker Identifier 89800
         Is Billable 0 - Execution is not billable.
         Resource Tier 100 - Basic Compute
         Resource Tier Memory 1024
         --
   EdgeWorkers JS executions S:89800
   Stage information S:89800
      Stage S - Sub-request
      EW ID 89800
High Precision Turnaround time5548
Request Pre-processing time2934
Midgress Time1492
Midgress Time2499
```

In the EW dashboard the performance metrics all look fine. And the sample log lines do not suggest it is an EW problem - ie the High-Precision times do not seem to correspond to EW execution times or forward request/response times. Therefore we would like to understand why Ghost kept the EW SR pending, but  once it executed the request it was quiet prompt with low latency.
 
Surrounding cache.log can be found in lsg-gss3 /home/aiprabhu/cache-GIP2.22.239.156-202603221145-23.33.25.197-169-1774337464.gz
 
Etherpad logs [https://etherpad.akam.ai/p/F-CS-11066090] line 937 (old example without cache log), and 1332 - latest example with cache log above.
executive_summary: High RET for EW SR with low latency in EW stats - Ghost seems to be stalling before forwarding SR
resolution_description: The answer has been provided to the query raised.
creator: Rahul Sawarkar

summary: GRN is missing  the request id when the request is triggering error:url-too-large
description: Reported in the SECESC-30762 when the url is larger than the max allowed length we are aborting the request with url-too-large error, in this case the GRN is missing the request id.
creator: Kristine Poghosyan

summary: FOM tuning guidelines ahead of IPL
description:
The IAT has asked us to engage ERE on a few points which would help us better prepare for the upcoming IPL events. 

As suggested in *ERE-3237* backup maps for the existing Web/API Maps ({*}Ch7, Ch42, S250{*}) have been added to better handle potential traffic spikes.

The back map for *ch7 is ch8* and backup map for {*}ch42 & s250 is ch43{*}. The split between ch42 and s250 remains 50:50. This change is in staging and is expected to be fully implemented before IPL. 

All FOM changes in place using the previous [guideline|https://collaborate.akamai.com/confluence/spaces/EPRE/pages/1345968190/FOM+Reduction+-+IN+Event+Delivery+-+Guidelines+for+NOCC+Tier3] provided in *ERE-3207* have been reverted. 

*Ask to ERE:*
We are seeking guidance from ERE to:
1) Revalidate whether any FOM tuning or optimizations are required for the India for primary maps looking ahead to IPL.
2) If FOM reduction is to be applied in similar scenarios can ERE please provide us with a similar guideline or instructions for doing so during Event Support.
resolution_description: Provided the guidelines and completed the proactive FOM reduction for regions part of e;s250 and a;chc1
root_cause: Preparation for IPL tournament based on the issues observed in T20 WC
creator: Tyler Deary
mitigation_plan: Proactive FOM reduction on regions serving spiky traffic
longterm_mitigation: Enhancement of a feature - using MPG ID as key rather than Pingpoint while constructing scorelist - this would help in spreading the forward requests more evenly/balanced

summary: Sharp Corporation- EIPB migration across contracts (within account) on 15th April 2026
description:
Migration Date: 15th April, 2026 10:00 AM (IST)
 
SOURCE 
Account Name: Sharp Corporation
Account ID:1-6KO939
Contract ID: V-4ZW1E5N
---
DESTINATION
Account Name: Sharp Corporation (within the same account)
Account ID:1-6KO939
Contract ID: V-4ZW1E1T
 
EIPB Configuration: 
prod2-limelightcdn-hro.jp.sharp
stage2-limelightcdn-hro.jp.sharp
executive_summary:
The migration project involved moving EIPB (Edge IP Binding) configurations for Sharp Corporation from a source contract to a destination contract within the same account. The migration was successfully executed on April 15, 2026, as scheduled. The activity ensured that specific edge hostnames and their associated CP codes were correctly mapped to the new contract ID to maintain service continuity and billing accuracy.
Impact: No customer-facing downtime (Zero Impact).
resolution_description: The migration was completed via a controlled database script execution (DBMC),Pre-migration checks confirmed the existence of EIPB line items in the destination contract.,SQL queries were run to identify the unique IDs (134033, 134034) for the relevant hostnames.A DBMC request (ID: 5e785ea0...) was submitted and approved to update the asapmstr.acg_objects table.The master_acg_id and acg_id were successfully changed from V-4ZW1E5N to V-4ZW1E1T.
root_cause: The requirement for this activity was not a "fault" but a business-driven migration. The customer needed to consolidate or reassign their EIPB resources (prod2-limelightcdn-hro.jp.sharp and stage2-limelightcdn-hro.jp.sharp) from an old contract to a new one. In the internal system (ASAPMSTR), these objects were hard-linked to the source contract ID, necessitating a manual database update (DBMC) to re-parent them.
creator: Ritu Vijayraj
mitigation_plan: The migration date was verified against the Moratorium Calendar to avoid conflicts with global freezes.Data was extracted both before and after the update to ensure no data loss occurred during the UPDATE command.The manual DBMC served as a workaround for the lack of a self-service migration UI in the EIPB portal.
longterm_mitigation: We are already having a EIPB Migration tool in a resource migration tool but due to some ongoing issues reporter not able to use that and went for the traditional mitigation plan

summary: Intermittent 504s and 503s spike mainly from RJIL Edge regions
description:
Issue summary: The customer has reported a sudden spike in 503 and 504 errors, accompanied by high Turn Around Time (TAT), impacting both SSAI MT and SSAI DAI services during the "Wheel of Fortune" live stream. 

As per the internal monitoring tool we noticed that impacted ISP is Reliance Jio Infocomm Limited with a VPFT spike of 29.89 %


Traffic peak 
[https://grafana-apj.trafficpeak.live/goto/4rebUy5vg?orgId=549]
Internal traffis peak 
[https://grafana-apj.trafficpeak.live/goto/F1EaUs5DR?orgId=549]

[https://www.nocc.akamai.com/miniurl/?id=2993b]

When the issue happened outstanding regions in RJIL were 30622, 34604 and there is a nocc ticket opened as well.

[https://issues.deploy.akamai.com/cgi-bin/view.cgi?issue_id=B-W-1CL5XTF] 

As per nocc the packet loss was due to multiple fiber cuts in the NLD network between Vijayawada and Bhubaneshwar. 

Currently we are asking for more detailed information from the ISP and would like to confirm if this would be okay to mr suspend the region 30622 as the customer has major events during this weekend. 
{code:java}
$ dig sonymtpristinenew.akamaized.net | grep CNAME
sonymtpristinenew.akamaized.net. 891 IN    CNAME    a799.dscz.akamai.net.

mr9274 "Dual-stack compliance edge map for z.akamai.net. Always returns A and AAAA."{code}
[https://etherpad.akam.ai/p/F-CS-11122891] has more information with logs.

Can you please review this case and suggest if this would be okay to suspend the region 30622 to see if this mitigates the issue and any other prevention plans?
root_cause: AANP backbone (NLD) congestion issue.
creator: Dimitris Kim
mitigation_plan: A public TD (ch2) was suggested to keep the traffic from AANP regions to avoid their backbone (NLD).
longterm_mitigation: NA

summary: Connection failures for MT servers caused by high flits
description:
*Issue summary -*

Customer reported that they have experienced intermittently connectivity issue which resulted in 504 timeout on their two leg service configuration.

This service has configured spanning two legs with 1 CPcode.
 - Leg #1 : End user => Akamai => Origin (AWS, Media-Tailor called EMT)
 - Leg #2 : EMT => Akamai => Origin

*Symptoms / Problem Statement -*

The customer is experiencing intermittent connectivity issues resulting in 504 Gateway Timeouts on Leg #1. The customer reports that the 504 responses from the Origin on Leg #1 are directly caused by connection delays and exceptions occurring on Leg #2 (toward the Akamai edge).

*Technical Findings & Evidence -* 

1. Based on log analysis and region load graphs from multiple timeframes (03/25 and 03/29), the root cause points to severe resource exhaustion on specific edge regions handling Leg #2 traffic:

2. Impacted Regions: The edge IPs shared by the customer and observed in egress/ingress logs are heavily concentrated in regions r48026, r44521, r43979, r41271, and r52255.

3. Resource Exhaustion (High CPU/Flit): Cache logs show extreme resource spikes in these regions, with CPU usage frequently hitting 90-99% and Flit levels exceeding 100% (peaking over 150% in some instances).

4. SSL Handshake Failures: There is a massive correlation between the high load and SSL handshake failures. Cache logs reveal thousands of aborted and timeout handshake results between the EMT clients and the Leg #2 edges.

5. Ghost Log Confirmation: When connections to the edges are successful, ghost logs show no significant response time delays. The 504 errors on Leg #1 are exclusively stemming from the dropped/timed-out SSL connections on Leg #2 due to edge overload.

*Questions -* 

1. High flits mitigation: Based on the logs and region load graphs, confirm if there are any additional actions or configurations we can apply to mitigate the connectivity issues specifically caused by these high flits spikes.

2. FOM Adjustment: Please confirm if it is possible to adjust it for region r44521 (currently at 0.8) to better handle the potential impact of these high flit loads. Some regions already have lowered FOM but the issue also happened to those regions.
{code:java}
=> Additionally, 3 of those regions were already lowered FOM.

[dbae@lsg-gss17 ~/case/F-CS-11122260/0329]$ date; agg map "select region,flit_resource,flit_overflow_mark from m2RegionOverflowMarks where region in (48026,44520,44521,43979,52255,41271)"
Sun Mar 29 13:47:54 UTC 2026
region  flit_resource  flit_overflow_mark
------  -------------  ------------------
41271              2                0.68
43979              2                0.68
44520              2                 0.8
44521              2                 0.8
48026              2                0.64
52255              2                 0.8
 
6 rows selected {code}
3. Load distribution methods : The client (AWS) needs to distribute loads across a wider variety of edge IPs and regions to safely handle high RPS requests. Pre-spreading within the Region to balance loads would work in this case?

4. Previoulsy other customers who are using MT(Media Tailor) servers had issues like read timeout, MPE, in-country mapping and so on. Howerver in this case it looks like most of issues happened when there were connection failurs like ssl connection error in those regions with high flits. Their workflow is like 'end user - edge - MT - EMT - edge - customer's origin'. Is there any known issue with EMT regarding the connection failure?

 

[https://etherpad.akam.ai/p/F-CS-11122260-ER] has more detailed information.
executive_summary:
[8-Apr-26]
Issue : 5xx on MT flow for Samsung TV plus {SI - Phase 2}
Summary : Moving the top hostname to w40 helped with reduction in the errors seen . So now we are working towards splitting the EMT traffic (leg 2) to other hostname so as to be able to leverage API shards to spread MPD load coming from AWS Route 53 
Blocker: Waiting for approval from PM to use API shards, needed data was provided today . (04/8/26)
root_cause:
Traffic inflow surge from MT AWS in the second leg caused regions to be overwhelmed and the 5xx due to connect timeout . Same hostname was used for leg  1 and leg2 , below is the flow.

Leg #1 : End user => Akamai => Origin (AWS, Media-Tailor called EMT)
Leg #2 : EMT => Akamai => Origin
creator: Dimitris Kim
mitigation_plan: Moving leg 2 - MT calls to different hostname and use the APIx shards to handle the traffic better.
longterm_mitigation: https://track.akamai.com/jira/browse/ERECSO-73

summary: 503 due to FRP denied from r48175
description:
Customer reported intermittent 503 errors from March 6th. We collected a recent spike of 503s and reviewed the logs. Reviewing them, we see the error was due to ERR_FRP_DENIED|fwdFRPDeny
 !image-2026-03-31-17-39-02-790.png|width=100%! 

For the specific client IP they reported, there were 82 instances of 503s due to FRP denial. Within a 5-min period, there were 694 requests with 503 response to client. 

{code:java}
From 2026-03-30T01:15 to 2026-03-30T01:40
CP codes: 866126
Filters: c_ip matches 99.25.13.11

    420 200 -
     82 503 ERR_FRP_DENIED|fwdFRPDeny
      4 302 -
      1 200 ERR_POC_FWD_OBJ_TOO_SMALL


From 2026-03-30T01:20 to 2026-03-30T01:25
CP codes: 866126

2411112 200 -
   8776 206 -
   1972 301 -
   1061 200 ERR_CLIENT_ABORT|general
    694 503 ERR_FRP_DENIED|fwdFRPDeny
    587 302 -
    348 504 -
    341 200 ERR_POC_FWD_OBJ_TOO_SMALL
     90 206 ERR_CLIENT_ABORT|general
     32 403 -
     29 503 -
     24 200 ERR_POC_FWD_NOT_206|validatePocRangeResponse
     11 409 -
      3 500 -
      1 301 ERR_PURGE_SEQ_OUT_OF_DATE|httpReadReply
{code}

Further investigation showed that all the errors were from IP 23.47.125.164, part of region 48175. We see most of the forward hostnames were MCH, while some of them were chf and peer. 

{code:java}
[hbelaval@lsg-gss3 ~]$ zcat CP866126-202603300120-23.211.12.32-199-1774941648.gz | cc_log_convert  | awk '$2=="r" && !($16~/[fGKPX]/) && !($11~/^(10\.|127\.0\.0\.1)/) && $15!="-" && $14=="503" {print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn 
    694 23.47.125.164


[hbelaval@lsg-gss3 ~]$ zcat CP866126-202603300120-23.211.12.32-199-1774941648.gz | awk '$2=="f" && ($19!~/o/) && $30=="ERR_FRP_DENIED|fwdFRPDeny" {print $1}'| sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
    747 23.47.125.164

[hbelaval@lsg-gss3 ~]$ zcat CP866126-202603300120-23.211.12.32-199-1774941648.gz | awk '$2=="f" && ($19!~/o/) && $30=="ERR_FRP_DENIED|fwdFRPDeny" {print $24}'| sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
    678 mch-ff-lo.akasrg.akamai.com
     22 a187.chf.akamai.net
     16 10.47.125.167
      4 a65.chf.akamai.net
      4 a63.chf.akamai.net
      4 a164.chf.akamai.net
      4 a100.chf.akamai.net
      2 a223.chf.akamai.net
      2 a179.chf.akamai.net
      2 10.47.125.172
      2 10.47.125.171
      2 10.47.125.169
      2 10.47.125.168
      2 10.47.125.166
      1 a361.chf.akamai.net

[hbelaval@lsg-gss3 ~]$ zcat CP866126-202603300120-23.211.12.32-199-1774941648.gz | awk '$2=="f" && $19!~/o/ && $30~/ERR_FRP/ {print $3}' | time_convert | cut -d : -f 1-2 | sort | uniq -c
    429 2026/03/30-01:20
    171 2026/03/30-01:21
     40 2026/03/30-01:22
     33 2026/03/30-01:23
     74 2026/03/30-01:24
{code}

Sample request shows request was meant to go to MCH but didn't go forward due to FRP. This resulted in 503 as mentioned above. 

{code:java}
[hbelaval@lsg-gss3 ~]$ zcat CP866126-202603300120-23.211.12.32-199-1774941648.gz | grep "18c490e2"
23.47.125.164 f 1774833894.000 - 0 0 0 4 0 0 - 2600:1700:2ddf:f810:4924:7b24:f579:5d31 GET /=/1398/866126/365d/d2n1lzc94paqxz.cloudfront.net/tm/840a24ccd7c75076211d060179b3ad0c64fdc6ca/5ac334f1-82de-4a9e-8d70-9ed360a0/asset_audio_128_0init.mp4 0 - - - pa0tM 0 - - 0 mch-ff-lo.akasrg.akamai.com 0 0 - - 18c490e2 ERR_FRP_DENIED|fwdFRPDeny - 78 78 - - - - - 0 - 110013164;-;-;8872;0.0;- 0-0 m5a78b340.-1.n.g:1d473a58 - - - a556880 - 0 0 - e9c9d0e6.42f - - mid=1001;mtse=g|0;aid=819105;ulen=0:0;pin=Mav|amd;ssl=0::::1d;aidv=16;frp=d|i|d|100;netp=;fp=964;rmap=dscv;mle=20370;trno=0;vcd=4988 - 2296.mch-ff-lo-sr1786.akasrg.akamai.com 0 - L1:L1:::s - - sr-vod-dai-frndly.akamaized.net -
23.47.125.164 S 1774833893.957 39 42 5 0 282 282 944 2600:1700:2ddf:f810:4924:7b24:f579:5d31 GET 1398 866126 d2n1lzc94paqxz.cloudfront.net 503 236 V text/html - D 1 445 589 - - - - oP sga8 tm/840a24ccd7c75076211d060179b3ad0c64fdc6ca/5ac334f1-82de-4a9e-8d70-9ed360a0/asset_audio_128_0init.mp4 ERR_FRP_DENIED|fwdFRPDeny sr-vod-dai-frndly.akamaized.net ^ ^ Roku/DVP-15.1%20(15.1.4.3321-91) ^ 18c490e2 - - 31536000 ^ ^ - - ^ - - ^ - ^ - - mid=1001;mtse=g|4294967295;dev=w:generic_roku|1;pcf=//@tcp/26400F780205A0481305+0\//@tls/f0c81df8c95f4140;sn=36015||36015|;aid=819105;gd=//0@0;cw=//1/1::0/cw-d-us/1;dstr=//DS/50879/0;pin=Mav|amd;alpn=http/1.1;aidv=16;fp=964;mle=16591;trno=0;vcd=4988;icp=no-rec - - - 1398.dscv 724:82285:0:0 L1:L1:s:L1::s - - - 16e79024 - - 156 156 9226 4517 1017 - - - 2600:1402:9800:41::172f:7da7|1|2|1|1398|8439|31959|29|0 - - ConnL=43;
{code}

Cache.log shows many G2G requests across multiple hostnames, with a Linode hostname having 354k active requests. 

{code:java}
testgrp@23.47.125.164:~$ /a/sbin/arf -d 1774833894 /a/logs/cache.log | xargs zstdgrep -h "frp" | less
1774833823.645|7f280bcd8640|               0|       0|148| 1|N| 6088| frp::ForwardMonitor::buildActiveSet(): Top forward hosts: su-na.us-ord-1.linodeobjects.com (G2G) with 354399 active requests; bellhappy.download.akamai.com (G2G) with 14059 active requests; d2n1lzc94paqxz.cloudfront.net (G2G) with 14003 active requests; storprodsrweather-internetrouting.blob.core.windows.net (G2G) with 5539 active requests; tvecmaftcm2.download.akamai.com (G2G) with 745 active requests; s7fmisg1.scene7.com (G2G) with 739 active requests; assets.prod0-promotions-prod.2kct.onl (G2G) with 510 active requests; dedc0b3cdf78a9446ce2d79c6e721330.egress.mediapackage-vod.us-east-1.amazonaws.com (G2G) with 437 active requests; deadtarget.storage.googleapis.com (G2G) with 313 active requests; rsg-fivem-forum-assets.s3.dualstack.us-west-2.amazonaws.com (G2G) with 260 active requests;
1774833823.645|7f280bcd8640|               0|       0|148| 1|N| 6090| frp::ForwardMonitor::buildActiveSet(): Active set: d2n1lzc94paqxz.cloudfront.net (G2G); bellhappy.download.akamai.com (G2G); su-na.us-ord-1.linodeobjects.com (G2G);
1774833853.844|7f280bcd8640|               0|       0|148| 1|N| 6088| frp::ForwardMonitor::buildActiveSet(): Top forward hosts: su-na.us-ord-1.linodeobjects.com (G2G) with 354399 active requests; bellhappy.download.akamai.com (G2G) with 14059 active requests; d2n1lzc94paqxz.cloudfront.net (G2G) with 14003 active requests; storprodsrweather-internetrouting.blob.core.windows.net (G2G) with 5542 active requests; tvecmaftcm2.download.akamai.com (G2G) with 756 active requests; s7fmisg1.scene7.com (G2G) with 739 active requests; dedc0b3cdf78a9446ce2d79c6e721330.egress.mediapackage-vod.us-east-1.amazonaws.com (G2G) with 718 active requests; assets.prod0-promotions-prod.2kct.onl (G2G) with 510 active requests; deadtarget.storage.googleapis.com (G2G) with 313 active requests; rsg-fivem-forum-assets.s3.dualstack.us-west-2.amazonaws.com (G2G) with 260 active requests;
1774833853.844|7f280bcd8640|               0|       0|148| 1|N| 6090| frp::ForwardMonitor::buildActiveSet(): Active set: d2n1lzc94paqxz.cloudfront.net (G2G); bellhappy.download.akamai.com (G2G); su-na.us-ord-1.linodeobjects.com (G2G);
1774833883.845|7f280bcd8640|               0|       0|148| 1|N| 6088| frp::ForwardMonitor::buildActiveSet(): Top forward hosts: su-na.us-ord-1.linodeobjects.com (G2G) with 354399 active requests; bellhappy.download.akamai.com (G2G) with 14059 active requests; d2n1lzc94paqxz.cloudfront.net (G2G) with 14003 active requests; storprodsrweather-internetrouting.blob.core.windows.net (G2G) with 5543 active requests; tvecmaftcm2.download.akamai.com (G2G) with 766 active requests; s7fmisg1.scene7.com (G2G) with 739 active requests; assets.prod0-promotions-prod.2kct.onl (G2G) with 510 active requests; deadtarget.storage.googleapis.com (G2G) with 313 active requests; rsg-fivem-forum-assets.s3.dualstack.us-west-2.amazonaws.com (G2G) with 260 active requests; saypromo.sfo2.digitaloceanspaces.com (G2G) with 204 active requests;

{code}

As you can see the number of active requests didn't change within 60-seconds above. 
We checked the cache.log for the logs available and the number of active requests didn't change for almost 2 days. 

{code:java}
Very first cache.log entry has the same number
testgrp@23.47.125.164:~$ /a/sbin/arf -f 3d /a/logs/cache.log | xargs zstdgrep -h "frp" | head -1
1774815076.444|7f280bcd8640|               0|       0|148| 1|N| 6088| frp::ForwardMonitor::buildActiveSet(): Top forward hosts: su-na.us-ord-1.linodeobjects.com (G2G) with 354399 active requests; bellhappy.download.akamai.com (G2G) with 13180 active requests; storprodsrweather-internetrouting.blob.core.windows.net (G2G) with 3851 active requests; fb89a3b6273106930d55e9747de3d9d4.byteoversea.com (G2G) with 768 active requests; assets.prod0-promotions-prod.2kct.onl (G2G) with 482 active requests; s7fmisg1.scene7.com (G2G) with 426 active requests; deadtarget.storage.googleapis.com (G2G) with 276 active requests; tvecmaftcm2.download.akamai.com (G2G) with 258 active requests; nmnanaori.s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com (G2G) with 194 active requests; saypromo.sfo2.digitaloceanspaces.com (G2G) with 179 active requests;


testgrp@23.47.125.164:~$ /a/sbin/arf -f 1d /a/logs/cache.log | xargs zstdgrep -h "frp::ForwardMonitor" | grep "su-na.us-ord-1.linodeobjects.com" | tail -2
1774977279.445|7f280bcd8640|               0|       0|148| 1|N| 6088| frp::ForwardMonitor::buildActiveSet(): Top forward hosts: su-na.us-ord-1.linodeobjects.com (G2G) with 354399 active requests; bellhappy.download.akamai.com (G2G) with 14059 active requests; d2n1lzc94paqxz.cloudfront.net (G2G) with 14003 active requests; storprodsrweather-internetrouting.blob.core.windows.net (G2G) with 9496 active requests; rsg-fivem-forum-assets.s3.dualstack.us-west-2.amazonaws.com (G2G) with 5231 active requests; tvecmaftcm2.download.akamai.com (G2G) with 2202 active requests; storprodkhweather-internetrouting.blob.core.windows.net (G2G) with 1951 active requests; s7fmisg1.scene7.com (G2G) with 1423 active requests; assets.prod0-promotions-prod.2kct.onl (G2G) with 1237 active requests; kayzen-bucket.us-ord-1.linodeobjects.com (G2G) with 1174 active requests;
1774977279.445|7f280bcd8640|               0|       0|148| 1|N| 6090| frp::ForwardMonitor::buildActiveSet(): Active set: d2n1lzc94paqxz.cloudfront.net (G2G); bellhappy.download.akamai.com (G2G); su-na.us-ord-1.linodeobjects.com (G2G);


For almost 2 days, it's the same number of active requests. 
[seselvan@lsg-gss6 ~]$ unixtime 1774815076
Sun Mar 29 20:11:16 2026
[seselvan@lsg-gss6 ~]$ unixtime 1774977279
Tue Mar 31 17:14:39 2026
{code}

Checking the logs around the last FRP error, we don't see any requests for that Linode hostname though.

{code:java}
testgrp@23.47.125.164:~$ /a/sbin/arf -d 1774977279 /a/logs/ghost.ddc.log | xargs zstdgrep -h "su-na.us-ord-1.linodeobjects.com" | awk '$1=="f" {print $14, $29}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head
testgrp@23.47.125.164:~$
{code}
 
[FRP tool|https://tools.gss.akamai.com/monitor/origin/frp/] shows 2 instances for that Linode hostname yesterday. 
{quote}
su-na.us-ord-1.linodeobjects.com
Mar 30, 2026 - 22:00:01 Ghost to Ghost: 23.47.125.168 (94/20) CPcode: 1984016;
Mar 30, 2026 - 21:55:02 Ghost to Ghost: 23.47.125.168 (49/20) CPcode: 1984016;
{quote}

We escalated this issue to NOCC via [B-V-6G27CNZ|https://issues.deploy.akamai.com/cgi-bin/view.cgi?issue_id=B-V-6G27CNZ] and following is their response. 
{quote}
As you had mentioned, FRP is active as of now for the domains(including our NS and Compute object hostnames) [1] ,interestingly all of them are G2G (ghost-to-ghost) . It seems this has been active since the 29th [2] and seems to have been triggered due to excessive in-progress requests in queue . Several domains under *frndly.akamaized.net are being denied due to the said reason [3].

This isnt specific to the machine 23.47.125.164, the entire region is currently facing the onslaught of FRP trying to keep the in-progress requests in control . Even CRP seem to not tag any ARLIDs, its possible this isn't meeting any of the defined criteria. The FRP triggers are seen across the region [4] for various domains, with the common factor being G2G . This seems to be a GFI region (GHost-fault-Tolerance) and has CPG/OCG and EPG enabled and monitored by ghostmon , not sure if this region is optimised to serve traffic.

I'd suggest getting this vetted by Engineering team . If this is causing a problem , you could perhaps perform a MR suspension
{quote}

This region is being [maprule suspended|https://www.nocc.akamai.com/nert/view.cgi?mode=suspension&id=10043] as we speak due to on-going issue, especially during peak hours. 

Reviewing [alerts for this machine|https://alerts.akamai.com/cgi-bin/reporter/alertreporter?__option_summary_collate_by=percent&__option_all_attributes=no&__option_over_time_groupby=hour&__option_over_time_groupby_definition=no&__option_over_time_use_graph=yes&__option_most_frequent_groupby=Alert+Instance+Key&__option_most_frequent_use_defs=no&__option_most_frequent_threshhold=50&report_type=alert_history&__option_p1_thres=1&__option_p2_thres=20&__option_p3_p5_thres=50&__exact_Definition+ID=exact&__exact_Alert+Name=substring&__exact_Ecor+Number=exact&__exact_Region+Number=exact&__pattern_Machine+IP=23.47.125.164&__exact_Machine+IP=exact&__exact_Alert+Instance+Key=exact&__exact_Owner+Email=substring&__exact_Ticket+ID=exact&__exact_AMS+Alert+Definition+ID=exact&__dynamic_filter_=Install+Group&__exact_alert_data_value_1_=exact&__exact_alert_data_value_2_=exact&__exact_alert_data_value_3_=exact&__exact_alert_data_value_4_=exact&gmtoffset=-14400&__time_options_=__use_start_&__month_From=3&__day_From=24&__year_From=2026&__hour_From=20&__min_From=33&__month_Until=3&__day_Until=31&__year_Until=2026&__hour_Until=20&__min_Until=33&__time_relation_Age=1&__time_unit_Age=Days&output_format=HTML&useNewWindow=on&timezone=gmt&datasource=Production&Action=Create%20Report], we see some GFI process rolls, so wondering if the FRP denials are due to GFI settings in this region as identified by NOCC. 

Etherpad with analysis: https://etherpad.akam.ai/p/F-CS-11139562

Ack: Can we review the FRP errors in this region and take necessary steps to avoid customer impact due to them?
executive_summary: Customer reported occasional 503 errors. Reviewing logs, we see FRP denials causing these 503s. The errors were coming from machines in r48175. The issue was escalated to NOCC, and they mentioned that the region is GFI and it needs to be vetted by engineering.
creator: Senthuran Selvanayagam

summary: Jio Hotstar - Lanuch of "Micro Dramas" - Config and Map review
description:
Jiostar is launching “*Microdrama*” a Short for video setup on 6th April. This is produced and curated by JioStar and is not UGC content.

The *Microdrama project* setup is cloned from existing VOD but optimized to use for shorts delivery.

The setup has been reviewed by Sachi (EA) and we implemented optimizations to metadata as suggested.

This will use a separate VOD domain, Setup details are documented [here|https://docs.google.com/document/d/1tIfXnTxXL-EHhjkN-gVxtRk5eKqPafaZot9rz7urrTA/edit?tab=t.0].

Confluence document [here|https://collaborate.akamai.com/confluence/spaces/GSSCUSTPAGES/pages/1345961135/Microdrama+Project].
resolution_description: Reviewed the config and recommended the Edge map change to dscw80
root_cause: ERE review for the config/maps before the launch of new type of series in JioHotstar app
creator: Aruna Uthayakumar
mitigation_plan: Reviewed the config and recommended the Edge map change to dscw80
longterm_mitigation: NA

summary: ERR_ACCESS_DENIED|vr-m error seen in customer traffic causing DoS
description:
Traffic report shows a significant spike in 403 responses during the timeframe (2026-03-30 14:00 to 14:15 UTC).

Based on log analysis, a high number of 403 status codes with ERR_ACCESS_DENIED|vr-m seen. This issue is not limited to a single CPCode and is impacting multiple CPCodes.

From the Grafana, CW Summed Bucket (cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg), a high number of denied hits were observed during the same timeframe. 


{code:java}
Denied on midgress parent -> CW with ERR_ACCESS_DENIED|vr-m

bucketname cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg 
keytype.cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg footprint
resource.cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg midgress_flits
loadlimit.cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg 145500Mf
denymethod.cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg haship
bucketloadtype.cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg summedunlisted
bucketdenytype.cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg threshold
bucketbasethreshold.cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg 60
bucketdeltathreshold.cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg 5
services.cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg W
loadallowlist.cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg VelvetRope_CW_SG
denyallowlist.cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg VelvetRope_All_W

testgrp@2.16.64.45:/a/logs$ /a/sbin/arf -d 1774879808 VelvetRope.log | xargs zgrep "DENY" | grep 'cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg'  | wc -l
139

testgrp@2.16.64.45:/a/logs$ /a/sbin/arf -d 1774879808 VelvetRope.log | xargs zgrep "DENY" | grep 'cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg' 
20260330 13:54:28 [3502381,3502390] 2 (VelvetRope/Controller.cc[888]): Summed: cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg (W) midgress_flits limit= 145500000000 load= 148216070144 (101.9%) demand= 148216070144 (101.9%) proposed= 1.83 previous= 0.00 slowed= 1.83 (1200/65535) DENY
20260330 13:54:40 [3502381,3502390] 2 (VelvetRope/Controller.cc[888]): Summed: cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg (W) midgress_flits limit= 145500000000 load= 151498113024 (104.1%) demand= 151498113024 (104.1%) proposed= 3.96 previous= 1.83 slowed= 3.96 (2594/65535) DENY
20260330 13:54:52 [3502381,3502390] 2 (VelvetRope/Controller.cc[888]): Summed: cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg (W) midgress_flits limit= 145500000000 load= 153876840448 (105.8%) demand= 156825223168 (107.8%) proposed= 7.22 previous= 3.96 slowed= 7.22 (4732/65535) DENY
....
.....
{code}


CW Summed Bucket : https://grafana.akam.ai/org426/dashboard/db/cloudwrapper-summed-buckets?from=1774872000000&to=1774882800000&var-bucketname=cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-sg&var-bucketname=cloud-wrapper-summed-flits-use&var-bucketname=cloud-wrapper-summed-bits-use&var-resource=All&var-keytype=All&var-key=All


Traffic Report : https://control.akamai.com/apps/reports/#/predefined/traffic-by-hostname-2?cpcodes=1954845&start=2026-03-30T00:00:00Z&end=2026-03-30T23:59:59Z&timezone=Greenwich

Key 33 hits the highest spike in terms of load - bytedance traffic

https://docs.akamai.com/ops/metadata/p4-1681/metadata/ump/ghostdir.quotas.data/fpdata.xml

{code:java}
<match:region.attribute value="regionset.W.monitoring.cloud_wrapper_sg">
<cache:fp-config name="cw-F-AC-2155425-sony-indiasgjp-d-sgjp">
<!--  MROM_343_2  -->
<mr-names>cw-d-sgjp</mr-names>
<fp-names>cw-F-AC-2155425-sony-indiasgjp</fp-names>
<id>27</id>
<priority>27</priority>
<count-by-percent>0.013531</count-by-percent>
<percent>0.002744</percent>
<store-logging.store-events>ACCESS</store-logging.store-events>
<store-logging.spatial-sampling-buckets>20</store-logging.spatial-sampling-buckets>
<store-logging.fields>1-4 8 10 14-18 21 23-26 28-35 40 45-46 48-50 53</store-logging.fields>
</cache:fp-config>
<cache:fp-config name="cw-F-AC-2077009-bytedance-d-sgjp">
<fp-names>cw-F-AC-2077009-bytedance</fp-names>
<mr-names>cw-d-sgjp</mr-names>
<percent>20.491799999999998</percent>
<priority>33</priority>
<id>33</id>
<max-idle-lifetime-strategy>retain-past</max-idle-lifetime-strategy>
<store-logging.store-events>ACCESS</store-logging.store-events>
<store-logging.spatial-sampling-buckets>20</store-logging.spatial-sampling-buckets>
<store-logging.fields>1-4 8 10 14-18 21 23-26 28-35 40 45-46 48-50 53</store-logging.fields>
</cache:fp-config>
{code}

It seems platform detected midgress traffic exceeding the allowed limit and VelvetRope enforced rate limiting and/or triggered aggressive cache eviction to protect Cloud Wrapper resources.
As a result, cache content was purged, reducing disk usage and object count, while traffic (flits) spiked due to increased cache misses and origin fetches?

Requesting ERE to confirm the root cause and what prevention steps can be taken to avoid this issue from happening again as customer is concerned about recurrence?

Analysis in
https://etherpad.akam.ai/p/F-CS-11156651
resolution_description: Mitigation is under discussion with the CW Product team, focusing on either increasing the SG VR bucket max deny threshold to 100 or deploying a dedicated VR bucket for TikTok to prevent impact on other footprints during future spikes.
root_cause:
VR denials occurred on SG CW due to a flit load spike in TikTok cpcodes.

Since TikTok contributes more than 50% of CW traffic in SG, the VR bucket parameters were tuned to deny only TikTok traffic during such spikes. However, in this instance, the default max deny threshold (currently set at 80 per Footprint) for Tiktok footprint was reached, resulting in denials across all FPs to maintain demand within limits.
creator: Senthilkumar Rajendiran
mitigation_plan: Mitigation is under discussion with the CW Product team, focusing on either adding  the parameter for maximum keys to deny or deploying a dedicated VR bucket for TikTok to prevent impact on other footprints during future spikes.
longterm_mitigation: Anomaly detection VR feature to deny only the keys causing traffic spike anomalies

summary: Analyze Coupang Traffic on mercury.coupang.com
description:
Spin off from ERE-3269 
To review the traffic distribution on mercury.coupang.com:

Even with auto-sharding enabled, load appears to be concentrated on api1 only. 
Task: Verify the effectiveness of current sharding.
executive_summary:
16/Apr
This ticket was created to review traffic distribution on mercury.coupang.com, and verify the effectiveness of current sharding setup.

- two reporting issues were observed for this setup.

1. Internal serial misreporting due to a bug. The fix was pushed last week
https://track.akamai.com/jira/browse/MAPPINGENG-8373  

2. Load reporting on only 1 of EHNIDs at any given time. 
There is a maprule override in the config. IAT to review this.
resolution_description:
Internal serial misreporting due to a bug was fixed MAPPINGENG-8373
A maprule override tag in the config, for IAT to fix.
root_cause: To review the traffic distribution on mercury.coupang.com, and verify the effectiveness of current sharding setup.
creator: Thilaga Raju
mitigation_plan: Internal serial misreporting was corrected in dnsp-config for Coupang while the fix is scheduled for May - LOADBAL-1333
longterm_mitigation: N/A

summary: SSL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT causing buffering for Disney
description:
Account - Disney Streaming Services
CPcode - 869650 (eu-west-1 )
Etherpad - [https://etherpad.akam.ai/p/F-CS-11162072]

Customer reported rebuffering on eu-west-1 today between 14:07-14:45 UTC. We ran a session analysis on sample client IPs they provided us with and determined spikes in SSL_CONNECT_TIMEOUTs mainly isolated to 46020 were causing the issue. 
!image-2026-04-02-16-16-48-621.png|width=791,height=468!

Session analysis - Client IP 95.99.93.252
!Screenshot 2026-04-02 at 4.18.49 PM-1.jpg|width=1512,height=251!

Sample Longline from the session with rebuffering (see etherpad line 108 for trace)
{code:java}
2.17.160.82 f 1775138872.754 6047 0 1 0 6151 0 0 10.17.160.83 95.99.93.252 GET /=/10038477/869650/365d/vod-ak-eu-west-1.mt.media.dssott.com/dvt3=exp=1775225222%7eurl=%2Fps01%2Fdisney%2F97b8f89a-9848-45cd-9393-8af206814894%2F%7epsid=b76a663e-5965-4d16-bc9e-b0b1cdbf9f58%7eaid=dc9c2366-44ad-4310-a264-58e8ffd5d50e%7edid=ee5c0a82-8f02-4b45-b44d-741a59ae6e1a%7ecountry=NL%7ecr=DE%7ekid=k02%7ehmac=3f83b6d235a03d5fa2edf1a3bdcc3ac19f1f3ce0b4204435d7d38df8058c3a39/ps01/disney/97b8f89a-9848-45cd-9393-8af206814894/r/b7c92b50-71e6-47ca-b9de-0b4f3ea1a891/fdbe-MAIN/02/16500K/00/01/12_072.mp4 0 - - TLSv1.3 g 0 - - 37a1a365 10.17.160.83 0 0 0 - 1c93c5b2 ERR_SSL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT|SSLTimeout - 58 58 - - - - - 0 2021 110029209;-;-;9057;-;-;0 - r7e5090d5c620.-35158894.p.g - - 522 33cf6880 - 0 0 J e9ce7838.b8689 %20Cg%20__slot30146____networkFF____levelNS____certCUS__ - mid=4000;mtse=g|0;sn=30146|;aid=1265513;icp=no-rec;ulen=0:0;hnd=1;m_slv=TLSv1.3;pin=Jav|amd;ssl=3001301::::17;aidv=44;netp=r;fp=964;rmap=dscv;trno=0;vcd=2850 - - 0 - L1:NS::: - - vod-akc-eu-west-1.media.dssott.com {code}
Back in December we raised ERE-3113 for a similar situation on Live for this customer, and it was mentioned the timeout value was too high for SSL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT's. The difference here being this is VOD and not Live. 

*Ask to ERE:*
We have been seeing SSL_CONNECT_TIMEOUTs causing spikes in the customers client side metrics regularly especially on eu-west-1. 
Would decreasing the 6s SSL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT with a similar tag help in alleviating some of the edge impact when spiky load causes SSL Timeouts?

*Tag*
_<security:ssl.handshake-timeout>3s</security:ssl.handshake-timeout>_

*Cache logs region 46020*
tdeary@lsg-gss7 - cache-GIP2.17.160.68-202604021400-23.33.25.197-700-1775157613.gz
*Ghost logs 14:06- 14:09 for CPcode 869650*
tdeary@lsg-gss7 - CP869650-202604021406-23.33.25.197-895-1775148084.gz

*Mapper*
[https://mapper.akamai.com/dnsp/#from%3D1775073677144%26to%3D1775160077144%26dataFrom%3D1775160045000%26useGMTTime%3Dtrue%26timeRange%3D%7B%22min%22%3A1775128900444.971%2C%22max%22%3A1775159378114.8738%7D%26regionId%3D46020%26service%3DW%26]

*Load* 
[https://lg.netarch.akamai.com/cgi-bin/loadgraphs?period=start1775134260end1775160420&region=46020&network=A&maprule=&display=maprule&ymax=&utcoffset=-5]
root_cause: TikTok's "unexpected" traffic and missing SSL connect timeout values in Disney's property.
creator: Tyler Deary
mitigation_plan: Suggested to check with TikTok's IAT about this unexpected traffic and optimize the property for timeout values.
longterm_mitigation: NA

summary: CloudWrapper configuration activation is pending "in progress" for 3 hours.
description:
Account: Century Games Pte. Ltd.

Account ID: F-AC-1290556

https://control.akamai.com/apps/cloud-wrapper/#/configurations

CloudWrapper configuration name: century-cw-group-2

CloudWrapper configuration name: Century-Game-US-west

MROM ID: 1985

https://mrom.akam.ai/app/v1/footprintConfig/1985

Splunk logs for trace id "c947b8437ce4362e"

: https://portalsplunk.akamai.com/en-US/app/search/search?earliest=1775174400&latest=1775260800&q=search%20%22c947b8437ce4362e%22&sid=1775215636.4976461_9CD559D9-47CE-46AF-AC48-84E91004AECF&display.page.search.mode=smart&dispatch.sample_ratio=1

Here we see the errors:

level: ERROR
logger: com.akamai.portal.media.fms.service.fpconfig.OrchestratorActivationService
message: Error Message: Handling DependentEndpointException - httpStatus:500, errorType:'INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR', instance:'/cloud-wrapper/error-instances/9d240ccb-2cca-4b6b-923b-6b0f8e4bb53e', message:'status 409, MROM error: Tuning parameters in previous version are still in ACCEPTED OR INQUEUE state', additionalJsonProblem:'[]'
pulsar_app: fms
executive_summary:
MROM ID: 8472

As per the audit trail, the status is waiting in the job step "MROM_PUBLISH_STATUS" with the message "[03 Apr 2026, 11:09 AM] Footprint with object id: 4355, Mrom ID: 8472 activation is in-progress"

UMP - https://ump.akamai.com/app/channels/ghostdir.quotas.data/tarballs/

MROM - https://mrom.akam.ai/app/v1/footprintConfig/8472

State - ACCEPTED in MROM

Splunk logs for trace id "a872228707844c78"

: https://portalsplunk.akamai.com/en-US/app/search/search?earliest=1775174400&latest=1775260800&q=search%20%22a872228707844c78%22&display.page.search.mode=smart&dispatch.sample_ratio=1

No errors seen in the above Splunk logs.

Ask : Need to check the reason for the delay in activation and help resolve the issue.

Note: There is one more CloudWrapper configuration in the same account, which is also pending "In Progress".
resolution_description: The total time taken for the configuration to become active was 7 hours 3 minutes 12 seconds.
root_cause: It took longer because there were multiple requests in the ongoing queue.
creator: Sabarinath Sundaram
mitigation_plan: No Mitigation from ERE Side required
longterm_mitigation: We can increase the bandwidth from MROM Side to handling the requests in a queue

We have updated log file location details and log analysis for each instance in the Etherpad -
[https://etherpad.akam.ai/p/APIX_Spikes]
 
*Ask:* Could ERE kindly review these frequent spikes in the APIX workflow and provide recommendations to address them, or suggest any possible optimizations for the APIX workflow?
resolution_description: Given its the same parent map, ch42 being used for SSAI manifest, traffic spikes are expected during event, so to make the apix config more resilient, recommended to include FA map to enable retries with higher chances on alternate path.
root_cause: Event spikes of Hotstar self-inflicting its own other configs
creator: Rahul Haridwari
mitigation_plan: Given its the same parent map, ch42 being used for SSAI manifest, traffic spikes are expected during event, so to make the apix config more resilient, recommended to include FA map to enable retries with higher chances on alternate path.
longterm_mitigation:
Handling the traffic spikes effectively in the platform.
Using MPG ID as key while constructing scorelist for Ghost MPGs to make sure regions are placed in different position for different MPGs of a ecor.

summary: Failure to move error when trying to move the cloud wrapper config
description:
Hello team,

The SAM performed the migration which included CW but he gets an error that it failed. Please see the screenshot and all prechecks were completed.

Please note that all the delivery assets were moved successfully. 

Source Account: Fox Entertainment
Source Contract ID: 3-3IFMD7
Cloud Wrapper Configuration Name: FoxDTC

Destination Account: Fox DTC
Destination Contract ID: V-4WBOUVT

 

!image-2026-04-07-23-01-40-832.png|width=1239,height=440!
executive_summary: During the migration of assets from the Fox Entertainment account to the Fox DTC account, the migration of a specific Cloud Wrapper (CW) configuration (FoxDTC) failed using the automated migration tool. The failure was caused by a system limitation that prevents the partial migration of CW configurations from a single source contract. The team successfully resolved this by manually recreating the configuration on the target account using a staged transition approach to minimize traffic impact and cache evictions.
resolution_description:
Since a tool-based migration was impossible for a single configuration, the team executed a manual transition:
Validated that the target contract (V-4WBOUVT) had sufficient purchased capacity (100 TB).
Created a new CW configuration on the Fox DTC account associated with a test property. Removed the specific ARLs from the legacy Fox Entertainment CW configuration.Immediately added those ARLs to the new Fox DTC CW configuration.
Pushed changes via UMP and confirmed traffic began flowing through the new footprint.
root_cause:
The automated Cloud Wrapper migration tool does not support "Partial Migration." If a source contract (3-3IFMD7) contains multiple CW configurations (in this case, three: FoxDTC, foxvdms, and FoxEntMedia), the tool cannot migrate just one. It requires an "all-or-nothing" migration of all CW objects under that contract. 
Manual recreation was initially blocked because the ARLs were already bound to the legacy configuration. Akamai controls prevent the same ARL from being associated with two different CW configurations simultaneously.
creator: Sharille Rodrigues
mitigation_plan:
The transition (Creation of a new CW configuration on account associated with a test property.and Removed the specific ARL & Immediately added those ARLs to the new Fox DTC CW configuration.) was recommended to be performed during off-peak hours.
During the "gap" period while UMP configurations were propagating, traffic was directed to use the default Cloud Wrapper footprint available for each map. This ensured continuity of service, though it carried a minor risk of increased origin pull due to temporary cache evictions.
longterm_mitigation: It will suggested clearer guidance for IATs and internal integration guides to explicitly highlight the "Partial Migration" limitation. And Establish a standard "Manual Move" checklist for CW configurations when full contract migrations are not feasible, ensuring QA validation is performed in a lab environment prior to production changes.

Reviewing logs, we identified OOC mapping for Frankfurt edge regions on the OIPACL API-a6068.akasrg.akamai.com map. Mapper tools also confirm high scores lately for origin mapIDs.
root_cause: SRMM mapping issue
creator: Senthuran Selvanayagam
mitigation_plan: SRMM origin pinging logic (port 443 from 80 for FF regions) was optimized and Edgescape data for Cloudfront was corrected.
longterm_mitigation: The ES data collection and SRMM pinging logic should be reviewed from a long term fix perspective.

summary: FRP effectiveness during March 29 hitload spike on Apple cpcode 847793
description:
One of Apple’s cpcodes experienced a hitload spike on March 29 which overloaded their origin, as well as our edge and cache-h layers.

Midgress hits exceeded edge hits:

https://www.nocc.akamai.com/miniurl/?id=29a48

We are told that FRP kicked in. We are wondering whether FRP was effective or not in protecting our midgress ghosts.
resolution_description: N/A consult
root_cause:
Apple had an influx of traffic on 3.29 ~1:00 utc 
ask was to see if FRP helped our td0 - results show it did not and only applied to the origin.
creator: Fabio Picconi
mitigation_plan: N/A - consult
longterm_mitigation: N/A - consult

Investigation would reveal exhaustion of retries (on the third party CDN) corelating with higher (than usual traffic) and high amount of FWD failures towards NS4 Origin with ERR_NO_FWD_HOSTS|fwdStartFail errors.

*Cache logs:* 

indicating correlation of ERR_NO_FWD_HOSTS with 

ERROR: not a cacheh selection-method: 3

&

fwdSetErr(): getTierNameAndType: unknown tier type

 

 
{code:java}
[mandreol@lsg-gss7 ~/ndr]$
zstdcat cache-GIP2.21.8.135-202603240000-23.33.25.197-0-1775643071.gz | grep "41323e4"
2.21.8.135 1774348896.739|7f48aaf80880|    7f48e43ea410| 41323e4|129| 1|U| 7057| guide::Util::getCachehHost(41323e4): ERROR: not a cacheh selection-method: 3
2.21.8.135 1774348896.739|7f48aaf80880|    7f48e43ea410| 41323e4|119| 1|E|12379| fwdSetErr(): getTierNameAndType: unknown tier type
{code}
 

 

*Observations:*

Selection Method 3 related error could be specific to:
https://track.akamai.com/jira/browse/GHOST-7453

<forward:cache-parent.sureroute.contact-multi-protocol-next-hop-on-failure>

 

FW Fail though to:
cache-parent.max-hops
https://track.akamai.com/jira/browse/ERE-2661

 

unkown tier to:
tier-order / definition
https://track.akamai.com/jira/browse/ERE-2121

 

Based on Metadatatrace I cannot make sense why MCH is there on the tier-order without getting surpressed , overwritten subsequently:

 
{code:java}
[mandreol@lsg-gss7 ~/ndr]$
nsh 23.59.213.137 metadata --mode=metadata3 https://001-dn028-dd-NDR-DD-mcdn-orig240486.akamaiorigin.net/ndr/2024/1217/TV-20241217-1219-4600.1080.mp4 > mdt_trace_results.txt
{code}
 
{code:java}
[mmaciasz@lsg-gss7 /home/mandreol/ndr]$ grep -i tier mdt_trace_results.txt
<cut>
X-Akamai-Metadata3: forward:cache-parent.metrocache.tier-order=MCH [S] [metrocache|metrocache-ff.xml] : [110]
X-Akamai-Metadata3: forward:cache-parent.metrocache.class.metrocache-tier-order=MCHFF [P] [metrocache|metrocache-ff.xml] : [119]
X-Akamai-Metadata3: forward:cache-parent.metrocache.class.metrocache-tier-order=MCHFF [P] [metrocache|metrocache-ff.xml] : [124]
X-Akamai-Metadata3: forward:cache-parent.metrocache.class.metrocache-tier-order=MCHESSL [P] [metrocache|metrocache-ff.xml] : [129]
X-Akamai-Metadata3: forward:cache-parent.metrocache.class.metrocache-tier-order=MCHESSL [P] [metrocache|metrocache-ff.xml] : [135]
{code}
 

based on the product design, past experience, state the flow for MultiCDN-CW for VOD should be:

Edge(uncacheable proxy)<>CW1<>CW2<>Origin.

 

We did also notice that ERR_NO_FWD_HOSTS are there whenever the machine contacting the NS4 Origin is not TD1, but for example TD0 or ICP part of the same forward chain.

 

*Ask:*

What is the reason behind and mitigation for ERR_NO_FWD_HOSTS ?

Broken tier definition order under the product baseline?

or <forward:cache-parent.sureroute.contact-multi-protocol-next-hop-on-failure>

should we try disabling <forward:cache-parent.sureroute.contact-multi-protocol-next-hop-on-failure> in first place?
executive_summary:
We see that the problem started around 4th Feb when the tag forward:cache-parent.sureroute.contact-multi-protocol-next-hop-on-failurewas enabled, 
We reproduced the issue and tried with setting the tag to off in advance metadata and it works
creator: Maciej Maciaszek

summary: ERR_FWD_SSL_HANDSHAKE | unable_to_set_supported_groups" errors at the Edge (r40486)
description:
Account Name : Fidelity Investments

CPCODE : 1705081 [digital.fidelity.com]

Etherpad: [https://etherpad.akam.ai/p/F-CS-11195446]

NOCC : #B-W-1D4CPJ7

 

Issue Summary:

MCO was alerted to a spike in HTTP 503 for the CPCODE : 1705081 (digital.fidelity.com) at around 03:05 UTC (04/11/26). Upon analysis, it was identified that one of the parent regions i.e. r40846 more specifically on one IP: 23.55.167.30 had *_ERR_FWD_SSL_HANDSHAKE|unable_to_set_supported_groups_* errors that lead to those 503 responses downstream at the edge.

While there was no major impact, we could see that these errors were intermittently have been causing spikes for Fidelity

!05_51_58.jpg|width=498,height=197!

Checking the same for 12-hour window, we can see the same occurring intermittently

We reached out to the NOCC under : *#B-W-1D4CPJ7* and they reached out further to T3 after the initial assessment provided below,

 
{noformat}
1] RHM looks good | load graph | high flits noticed - it is a regular pattern[
2] Mapper DNSP - Suspended 750 Fault noticed on 5 machines on region 40846
[3] Cache logs : sample checks on 2 machines shows high flit utilization and high  Average Manager Cycle
[4] Historical traces looks good[5] Nexus : Noticed maximum CPU usage | flit % @ 109%{noformat}
NOCC T3 provided us with the following set of details regarding the *IP : 23.55.167.30* mentioned in this ticket,

 

 
Reviewing the ticket , the error "{*}_ERR_FWD_SSL_HANDSHAKE|unable_to_set_supported_groups_{*}”  isn’t caused by the usual combination of load and suspend flags etc . The error seems to be specific to an encryption/elliptical curve used by OpenSSL binary .
 
This problem is still happening at this point with multiple hostnames /properties, and the same error shows up [1] , with Ghost logging errors about missing a group /curve “{*}_X25519_{*}" . At the time of writing, the error has occurred over 107345 times , repeating the same openssl-error code “{*}_168296710_{*}” . The error seems to be exclusive to G2G (Ghost-to-Ghost) requests /connections [3] and earliest occurrence of the error was on April 10th, ~15:53 UTC  [4] . 
 
This seems to be problem with Ghost or/and Ghost-OpenSSL as I was able to successfully finish the SSL handshake manually [5] . A possible root cause can be likely that the Ghost version ({*}_22.4.4.4)_{*} and Ghost-OpenSSL   ({*}_8.3.1.8.3)_{*} version are older than the rest of the peers in the region as seen in  [6] . You can confirm with engineering , if this behavior is a result of version mismatch on the machine vs the rest of its peers in region.
Note : This behavior seems to be local to this machine and have been suspended the machine as of now [7] at 06:45 UTC
